turns.user_message: Yeah, I guess so. I've been smoking for about 10 years now. I've tried to quit a few times, but it never sticks. I'm just really stressed out with my job, and cigarettes help me cope. But I know I need to quit. My friends and family are always on my case about it.
step2_rag_chunks: Support from friends, family, or healthcare professionals can improve the chances of successfully quitting smoking.
If you slip and smoke a cigarette, it does not mean you have failed. Many people attempt quitting several times before succeeding.
Nicotine addiction is both physical and psychological. Addressing both aspects can improve success in quitting.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the PLANNING phase. You have enough understanding of this patient. You can now gently introduce ONE relevant coping strategy or technique based on their specific triggers and past attempts. Keep it simple — one idea at a time, check if they are open to it.

turns.user_message: Honestly, I'm just not sure I believe all the hype about how bad smoking is for me. I mean, I'm healthy, I exercise, I eat well... I just don't think it's that big of a deal. And when I do try to quit, I get so anxious and irritable. It's like, I know I'm supposed to be doing something good for myself, but it feels like torture. And then there's the stress at work. I don't know how I'll deal with it without cigarettes.
step2_rag_chunks: Stress is a common trigger for smoking. Relaxation techniques such as deep breathing, meditation, or short walks can help manage stress without using tobacco.
Nicotine addiction is both physical and psychological. Addressing both aspects can improve success in quitting.
Quitting smoking reduces the risk of lung disease, heart disease, and many types of cancer.

Therapist: Hello, how are you? Welcome to EDosthi. I'm here to help you with tobacco cessation. Would you like to tell me about your tobacco use?

Patient: Yeah, I guess so. I've been smoking for about 10 years now. I've tried to quit a few times, but it never sticks. I'm just really stressed out with my job, and cigarettes help me cope. But I know I need to quit. My friends and family are always on my case about it.

Therapist: I understand. It sounds like you're feeling a bit stuck. Can you tell me more about what makes it hard for you to quit? What are some of the biggest challenges you face when you try to stop smoking?

Patient: Honestly, I'm just not sure I believe all the hype about how bad smoking is for me. I mean, I'm healthy, I exercise, I eat well... I just don't think it's that big of a deal. And when I do try to quit, I get so anxious and irritable. It's like, I know I'm supposed to be doing something good for myself, but it feels like torture. And then there's the stress at work. I don't know how I'll deal with it without cigarettes.

step7_response: It sounds like you're skeptical about the risks of smoking and feeling overwhelmed by the challenges of quitting. Can you tell me more about what makes you feel healthy and invincible, despite smoking for 10 years?

turns.user_message: I don't know, I just feel like I'm an exception or something. I'm not like those people who smoke a pack a day and have heart attacks in their 40s. I'm active, I take care of myself... I just don't think it'll happen to me. But at the same time, I know it's stupid to think that way. Deep down, I know I'm probably just in denial.
step2_rag_chunks: If you slip and smoke a cigarette, it does not mean you have failed. Many people attempt quitting several times before succeeding.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

Having even one person who knows you are trying to quit and supports you makes the process significantly easier. This is not about having someone watch over you â€” it is about having someone to call when the urge is strong, or someone who will not offer you a cigarette. Who in your life could be that person for you?

[Recent Conversation]
Therapist: Hello, how are you? Welcome to EDosthi. I'm here to help you with tobacco cessation. Would you like to tell me about your tobacco use?

Patient: Yeah, I guess so. I've been smoking for about 10 years now. I've tried to quit a few times, but it never sticks. I'm just really stressed out with my job, and cigarettes help me cope. But I know I need to quit. My friends and family are always on my case about it.

Therapist: I understand. It sounds like you're feeling a bit stuck. Can you tell me more about what makes it hard for you to quit? What are some of the biggest challenges you face when you try to stop smoking?

Patient: Honestly, I'm just not sure I believe all the hype about how bad smoking is for me. I mean, I'm healthy, I exercise, I eat well... I just don't think it's that big of a deal. And when I do try to quit, I get so anxious and irritable. It's like, I know I'm supposed to be doing something good for myself, but it feels like torture. And then there's the stress at work. I don't know how I'll deal with it without cigarettes.

Therapist: I see. It sounds like you're struggling with some skepticism about the health risks, and also worried about managing your stress and withdrawal symptoms. Can you tell me more about what you mean by "healthy smoker"? What makes you think you're not at risk for serious health problems?

Patient: I don't know, I just feel like I'm an exception or something. I'm not like those people who smoke a pack a day and have heart attacks in their 40s. I'm active, I take care of myself... I just don't think it'll happen to me. But at the same time, I know it's stupid to think that way. Deep down, I know I'm probably just in denial.
Current patient message:

step2_rag_chunks: Stress is a common trigger for smoking. Relaxation techniques such as deep breathing, meditation, or short walks can help manage stress without using tobacco.
Keeping your hands busy with activities such as drawing, writing, or using a stress ball can help reduce the urge to smoke.
Exercise can help improve mood and reduce nicotine cravings.
